mitre_attack: T1598.002 - Phishing for Information: Spearphishing Attachment
cwe: CWE-345 - Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
CWE-350 - Reliance on Reverse DNS Resolution for a Security-Critical Action

threat.title: Email Spoofing via SPF/DKIM/DMARC Bypass
threat.description: Business Email Compromise (BEC) attacks exploiting weak or misconfigured email authentication protocols (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) to impersonate executives, vendors, or trusted partners. Attackers leverage SPF soft fail (~all), DMARC policy p=none, DKIM replay attacks, and domain alignment failures to bypass email security filters and conduct CEO fraud, wire transfer scams, and credential harvesting campaigns. In 2024, 62% of phishing emails successfully bypassed DMARC checks, resulting in $16.6 billion in losses from BEC attacks.

attack.scenario:
1. Reconnaissance: Attacker queries target domain DNS for SPF/DMARC records, discovers SPF soft fail (~all) and DMARC p=none policy
2. Infrastructure Setup: Registers lookalike domain (e.g., cornpany.com vs company.com) or compromises legitimate SMTP server in SPF record
3. Email Crafting: Spoofs CEO/CFO email using display name deception ("John Smith <attacker@evil.com>") with authentic-looking signatures
4. SPF Alignment Bypass: Sends from unauthorized server relying on ~all soft fail to reach inbox instead of being rejected
5. Social Engineering: Urgent wire transfer request ($60K-$3.36M) marked confidential, targeting finance team during end-of-quarter
6. Financial Impact: Victim initiates fraudulent payment; funds transferred to attacker-controlled offshore accounts within hours
prerequisites: Target domain has SPF record ending with ~all (soft fail) instead of -all (hard fail)
DMARC policy set to p=none (monitoring only) or missing entirely
No DKIM signature validation or DKIM replay vulnerability
Employees lack security awareness training on BEC tactics
Email gateway lacks advanced threat protection (ATP) or AI-based anomaly detection

cve_references.description: Multi-tenant hosting vulnerability allowing authenticated sender to spoof shared domain identity, bypassing SPF/DKIM/DMARC policies
Shared SPF records in multi-tenant hosting allow malicious actors to abuse network authorization for sender email spoofing

real_world_breaches.impact: $60 million loss via fraudulent wire transfers; non-executive employee tricked by spoofed vendor email
real_world_breaches.root_cause: DMARC policy set to p=none allowed spoofed emails to reach inbox; lack of out-of-band payment verification

real_world_breaches.impact: $3.36 million stolen from state education funds; spoofed Pearson textbook publisher email requesting banking detail updates
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Email authentication bypass via display name spoofing; less than $750K recovered

real_world_breaches.impact: $500,000 loss when scammers hijacked construction invoice email thread and redirected four payments
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Thread hijacking via compromised legitimate email; SPF/DMARC failed to detect SMTP relay abuse

real_world_breaches.impact: $2.1 million AUD loss; government department tricked by financial institution impersonation
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Weak DMARC enforcement allowed spoofed banking emails to pass authentication checks

real_world_breaches.impact: Nation-state actors exploited p=none DMARC policies for spearphishing campaigns targeting critical infrastructure
real_world_breaches.root_cause: FBI IC3 report documented widespread abuse of weak email authentication for credential harvesting

bug_bounty_reports.summary: SPF soft fail misconfiguration allowed spoofing of internal executive emails for phishing simulation

security_research.title: 62% of Phishing Emails Bypassed DMARC Checks in H1 2024
security_research.key_finding: Of 17.8 million phishing emails, 62% successfully bypassed DMARC verification; p=none policies rendered authentication ineffective

security_research.title: FBI IC3 2024 Report: $16.6 Billion in BEC Losses
security_research.key_finding: 256,256 BEC complaints in 2024 with actual losses; 33% more effective than 2023; CEO fraud targeting wire transfers

security_research.title: DKIM Replay Attack Exploitation
security_research.key_finding: Attackers exploit high-reputation mailboxes to generate legitimate DKIM signatures, reuse them in spam campaigns bypassing filters

mitigations.title: Enforce DMARC p=reject Policy with SPF Hard Fail
mitigations.description: Implement strict DMARC enforcement (p=reject) combined with SPF hard fail (-all) to ensure unauthorized emails are rejected before reaching user inboxes. This eliminates the 62% bypass rate observed with p=none policies.

code_example.code:
# SPF Record Configuration (Hard Fail)
# Replace ~all with -all to reject unauthorized senders
v=spf1 ip4:203.0.113.0/24 include:_spf.google.com include:spf.protection.outlook.com -all

# DMARC Record Configuration (Strict Enforcement)
_dmarc.example.com. IN TXT "v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@example.com; ruf=mailto:dmarc-forensics@example.com; fo=1; adkim=s; aspf=s; pct=100; sp=reject"

# Explanation:
# p=reject: Reject emails that fail DMARC (not p=none or p=quarantine)
# adkim=s: Strict DKIM alignment (From header must exactly match d= domain)
# aspf=s: Strict SPF alignment (envelope-from must match From header)
# pct=100: Apply policy to 100% of failing emails
# sp=reject: Apply same policy to subdomains
# fo=1: Generate forensic reports for any authentication failure

# DKIM Record (2048-bit RSA key)
default._domainkey.example.com. IN TXT "v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=MIIBIjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAAOCAQ8AMIIBCgKCAQEA..."

# Verification Commands:
# dig TXT example.com | grep spf
# dig TXT _dmarc.example.com
# dig TXT default._domainkey.example.com

iso27001_controls: A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
PR.AC-7 - Users, devices, and other assets are authenticated
validation_steps: Audit current SPF records using 'dig TXT example.com' - verify ends with -all not ~all
Check DMARC policy: 'dig TXT _dmarc.example.com' - must show p=reject
Monitor DMARC aggregate reports (RUA) for 2 weeks before enforcing p=reject
Validate all legitimate email sources are in SPF include: statements
Test email deliverability from authorized servers after SPF/DMARC changes
Set up DMARC forensic reports (RUF) to investigate failures
Gradually roll out: p=none (week 1-2) -> p=quarantine (week 3-4) -> p=reject (week 5+)

mitigations.title: Implement DKIM Signing with Replay Attack Prevention
mitigations.description: Deploy DKIM email signing with 2048-bit RSA keys and implement timestamp validation to prevent DKIM replay attacks where attackers reuse legitimate signatures.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
validation_steps: Generate 2048-bit RSA DKIM key pair: openssl genrsa -out dkim-private.pem 2048
Extract public key: openssl rsa -in dkim-private.pem -pubout -out dkim-public.pem
Publish DKIM DNS TXT record: default._domainkey.example.com
Test DKIM signing: send test email and verify DKIM-Signature header exists
Validate with mail-tester.com or DKIM validator tools
Monitor DMARC reports for DKIM alignment failures
Rotate DKIM keys every 6-12 months

mitigations.title: Deploy Microsoft Defender for Office 365 / Google Workspace Security
mitigations.description: Enable advanced threat protection (ATP) features including impersonation detection, mailbox intelligence, and safe links to catch BEC attempts that bypass authentication.

code_example.code:
# Connect to Exchange Online
Connect-ExchangeOnline -UserPrincipalName admin@example.com

# Enable Anti-Phishing Policy with Impersonation Protection
New-AntiPhishPolicy -Name "Executive-Protection-Policy" `
  -EnableOrganizationDomainsProtection $true `
  -EnableTargetedUserProtection $true `
  -TargetedUsersToProtect "CEO@example.com","CFO@example.com","Finance@example.com" `
  -EnableMailboxIntelligence $true `
  -EnableMailboxIntelligenceProtection $true `
  -EnableSpoofIntelligence $true `
  -EnableUnauthenticatedSender $true `
  -AuthenticationFailAction Quarantine `
  -EnableFirstContactSafetyTips $true `
  -EnableSimilarUsersSafetyTips $true `
  -EnableSimilarDomainsSafetyTips $true `
  -EnableUnusualCharactersSafetyTips $true

# Apply policy to entire organization
New-AntiPhishRule -Name "Executive-Protection-Rule" `
  -AntiPhishPolicy "Executive-Protection-Policy" `
  -RecipientDomainIs "example.com" `
  -Priority 0

# Enable Safe Links (URL rewriting and time-of-click protection)
New-SafeLinksPolicy -Name "Company-SafeLinks" `
  -EnableSafeLinksForEmail $true `
  -ScanUrls $true `
  -DeliverMessageAfterScan $true `
  -EnableForInternalSenders $true `
  -TrackClicks $true `
  -AllowClickThrough $false

New-SafeLinksRule -Name "Company-SafeLinks-Rule" `
  -SafeLinksPolicy "Company-SafeLinks" `
  -RecipientDomainIs "example.com"

# Configure DMARC enforcement in Exchange Online
Set-HostedContentFilterPolicy -Identity Default `
  -SpamAction Quarantine `
  -HighConfidenceSpamAction Quarantine `
  -PhishSpamAction Quarantine `
  -HighConfidencePhishAction Quarantine `
  -BulkThreshold 6 `
  -MarkAsSpamBulkMail On `
  -IncreaseScoreWithImageLinks On `
  -IncreaseScoreWithNumericIps On

# Enable external sender tagging
Set-ExternalInOutlook -Enabled $true -AllowList @()

# Create Transport Rule for Financial Request Monitoring
New-TransportRule -Name "BEC-Wire-Transfer-Alert" `
  -SubjectOrBodyContainsWords "wire transfer","urgent payment","banking details","account change" `
  -SentToScope InOrganization `
  -FromScope OutsideOrganization `
  -BlindCopyTo "security@example.com" `
  -SetHeaderName "X-BEC-Alert" `
  -SetHeaderValue "Potential BEC - Verify sender authenticity"

# Verify configurations
Get-AntiPhishPolicy | Format-List Name,EnableOrganizationDomainsProtection,EnableSpoofIntelligence
Get-SafeLinksPolicy | Format-List Name,EnableSafeLinksForEmail,ScanUrls

nist_csf: PR.IP-12 - A vulnerability management plan is developed
validation_steps: Enable Defender for Office 365 in Microsoft 365 admin center
Configure impersonation protection for C-level executives
Test with simulated phishing campaigns using Attack Simulator
Monitor Security & Compliance Center for threat detections
Review quarantined messages daily for false positives
Train users to report suspicious emails using Report Message add-in

mitigations.title: Implement Out-of-Band Payment Verification Process
mitigations.description: Establish dual-factor verification for all wire transfers and payment changes using separate communication channels (phone call to known number, SMS, Slack).

code_example.code:
import os
from twilio.rest import Client
import hashlib
import secrets
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Initialize Twilio client
account_sid = os.environ['TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID']
auth_token = os.environ['TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN']
client = Client(account_sid, auth_token)

class PaymentVerificationSystem:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pending_verifications = {}
        self.from_phone = os.environ['TWILIO_PHONE_NUMBER']
    
    def initiate_wire_transfer_verification(self, transfer_details):
        """
        Trigger out-of-band verification for wire transfers > $10,000
        transfer_details: dict with amount, recipient, account_number, requester_email
        """
        amount = transfer_details['amount']
        recipient = transfer_details['recipient']
        requester = transfer_details['requester_email']
        
        # Generate unique verification code
        verification_code = secrets.token_hex(3).upper()  # 6-char code
        transfer_id = hashlib.sha256(f"{requester}{datetime.now()}".encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
        
        # Store verification details (expires in 30 minutes)
        self.pending_verifications[transfer_id] = {
            'code': verification_code,
            'details': transfer_details,
            'expires_at': datetime.now() + timedelta(minutes=30),
            'verified': False
        }
        
        # Retrieve CFO contact from employee directory
        cfo_phone = self._get_executive_phone('CFO')
        finance_manager_phone = self._get_executive_phone('Finance Manager')
        
        # Send SMS verification to CFO
        sms_body = f"""⚠️ WIRE TRANSFER VERIFICATION REQUIRED
        
Amount: ${amount:,.2f}
Recipient: {recipient}
Requester: {requester}
Verification Code: {verification_code}
        
Reply APPROVE {verification_code} to authorize.
Reply DENY {verification_code} to block.
Expires in 30 minutes.
        
If you did not request this transfer, contact security@example.com immediately."""
        
        try:
            # Send to primary approver (CFO)
            message = client.messages.create(
                body=sms_body,
                from_=self.from_phone,
                to=cfo_phone
            )
            print(f"Verification SMS sent to CFO: {message.sid}")
            
            # Also send email notification to finance team
            self._send_email_notification(transfer_details, verification_code)
            
            # Log the verification request
            self._log_verification_request(transfer_id, transfer_details)
            
            return {
                'status': 'pending',
                'transfer_id': transfer_id,
                'message': f'Verification code sent to CFO at {cfo_phone[-4:]}. Awaiting approval.'
            }
            
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Failed to send verification SMS: {e}")
            # Fail secure: block transfer if notification fails
            return {
                'status': 'blocked',
                'error': 'Verification system failure - transfer blocked for security'
            }
    
    def verify_code(self, transfer_id, code, action='APPROVE'):
        """
        Verify the OOB code received from executive
        """
        if transfer_id not in self.pending_verifications:
            return {'status': 'error', 'message': 'Invalid transfer ID'}
        
        verification = self.pending_verifications[transfer_id]
        
        # Check expiration
        if datetime.now() > verification['expires_at']:
            del self.pending_verifications[transfer_id]
            return {'status': 'expired', 'message': 'Verification code expired'}
        
        # Verify code
        if code.upper() != verification['code']:
            return {'status': 'error', 'message': 'Invalid verification code'}
        
        # Process approval/denial
        if action.upper() == 'APPROVE':
            verification['verified'] = True
            self._execute_wire_transfer(verification['details'])
            message = f"Wire transfer approved: ${verification['details']['amount']:,.2f} to {verification['details']['recipient']}"
        else:
            message = "Wire transfer DENIED by executive. Security team notified."
            self._alert_security_team(verification['details'], 'Transfer denied by executive')
        
        del self.pending_verifications[transfer_id]
        return {'status': 'completed', 'message': message}
    
    def _get_executive_phone(self, role):
        # In production: query employee directory / Azure AD
        executive_contacts = {
            'CFO': '+12025551234',
            'Finance Manager': '+12025555678'
        }
        return executive_contacts.get(role)
    
    def _send_email_notification(self, details, code):
        # Implementation: send email via SendGrid/SMTP
        pass
    
    def _execute_wire_transfer(self, details):
        # Implementation: integrate with banking API
        print(f"Executing wire transfer: {details}")
    
    def _log_verification_request(self, transfer_id, details):
        # Implementation: log to SIEM / CloudWatch
        print(f"LOG: Verification requested for {transfer_id}")
    
    def _alert_security_team(self, details, reason):
        # Implementation: send alert to SOC
        print(f"ALERT: {reason} - {details}")

# Example usage
verification_system = PaymentVerificationSystem()

# Finance employee receives email requesting wire transfer
transfer_request = {
    'amount': 60000,
    'recipient': 'Offshore Vendor LLC',
    'account_number': 'XXXX-1234',
    'requester_email': 'ceo@example.com'  # Could be spoofed!
}

# System triggers OOB verification
result = verification_system.initiate_wire_transfer_verification(transfer_request)
print(result)

# CFO receives SMS and replies with code
# verification_system.verify_code(transfer_id='abc123', code='4F2A1C', action='APPROVE')

iso27001_controls: A.13.2.1 - Information transfer policies and procedures
nist_csf: PR.AC-7 - Users, devices, and other assets are authenticated
validation_steps: Define wire transfer threshold requiring OOB verification (e.g., $10,000)
Establish callback numbers for all executives in secure directory
Configure automated SMS/call system for payment approvals
Train finance team to NEVER process large transfers without OOB confirmation
Test verification workflow with simulated BEC scenarios
Monitor verification bypass attempts and alert security team

indicators: Emails from external domains with executive display names (e.g., 'CEO John Smith <attacker@gmail.com>')
SPF authentication failures (received-spf: softfail or fail)
DMARC alignment failures (dmarc=fail) with p=none allowing delivery
Urgent language requesting wire transfers or payment changes
Requests to change vendor banking details via email
Emails sent outside business hours from 'executives' (time-zone mismatch)
Lookalike domains (e.g., cornpany.com vs company.com, rn vs m)
Missing DKIM signatures on emails claiming to be from internal domain
Reply-To address different from From address
Unusual geolocation of sender IP (e.g., executive 'in Nigeria' during business trip)
Multiple recipients BCC'd on financial request (unusual for legitimate exec communications)

log_queries.query:
// Detect emails with SPF/DMARC failures reaching inbox
EmailEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where SenderFromDomain == "example.com" // Internal domain spoofing
| where AuthenticationDetails.SPF == "fail" or AuthenticationDetails.SPF == "softfail"
| where AuthenticationDetails.DMARC == "fail" or AuthenticationDetails.DMARC == "none"
| where DeliveryLocation == "Inbox" // Bypassed quarantine
| where Subject contains "urgent" or Subject contains "wire" or Subject contains "payment"
| summarize AttemptCount = count(), UniqueRecipients = dcount(RecipientEmailAddress) by SenderFromAddress, SenderMailFromAddress, Subject, bin(Timestamp, 1h)
| where AttemptCount > 3
| order by AttemptCount desc
| project Timestamp, SenderFromAddress, SenderMailFromAddress, Subject, AttemptCount, UniqueRecipients

index=email sourcetype=office365:management:activity Operation=MailItemsAccessed OR Operation=Send
| eval is_external=if(match(SenderAddress, "@example\.com$"), "no", "yes")
| where is_external="yes" AND (match(Subject, "(?i)(wire|transfer|payment|urgent|banking|invoice)") OR match(P2Sender, "(?i)(ceo|cfo|president|director)"))
| eval spf_result=mvindex(split(AuthDetails, "spf="), 1)
| where spf_result IN ("fail", "softfail", "neutral")
| stats count as email_count, dc(Recipients) as unique_recipients, values(Subject) as subjects by SenderAddress, P2Sender, spf_result
| where email_count > 2
| sort - email_count
| table _time, SenderAddress, P2Sender, spf_result, subjects, email_count, unique_recipients

event.category: "email" AND event.action: "received"
AND (
  (email.from.domain: "example.com" AND NOT source.ip: (10.0.0.0/8 OR 172.16.0.0/12)) OR
  (email.authentication_results.spf.result: ("fail" OR "softfail") AND email.delivery_location: "inbox") OR
  (email.authentication_results.dmarc.result: "fail" AND email.subject: (*urgent* OR *wire* OR *payment*))
)
AND email.from.display_name: (*CEO* OR *CFO* OR *President* OR *Director*)

fields @timestamp, mail.source, mail.destination, mail.commonHeaders.subject, mail.messageId
| filter mail.tags.ses:configuration-set == "production-emails"
| filter (mail.spfVerdict.status == "FAIL" or mail.spfVerdict.status == "SOFTFAIL")
    or (mail.dkimVerdict.status == "FAIL")
    or (mail.dmarcVerdict.status == "FAIL")
| filter mail.commonHeaders.subject like /urgent|wire|transfer|payment|banking/i
| stats count() as FailureCount by mail.source, mail.spfVerdict.status, mail.dmarcVerdict.status
| sort FailureCount desc
| limit 50

SELECT
  event_info.timestamp,
  email_log_info.message_info.subject,
  email_log_info.message_info.source.address AS sender_address,
  email_log_info.message_info.destination[SAFE_OFFSET(0)].address AS recipient_address,
  email_log_info.authentication_info.spf AS spf_result,
  email_log_info.authentication_info.dmarc AS dmarc_result,
  email_log_info.message_info.rfc2822_message_id
FROM
  `workspace_logs.activity`
WHERE
  event_info.event_name = 'email_log_entry'
  AND DATE(event_info.timestamp) >= CURRENT_DATE() - 7
  AND (
    email_log_info.authentication_info.spf IN ('FAIL', 'SOFTFAIL', 'NEUTRAL')
    OR email_log_info.authentication_info.dmarc = 'FAIL'
  )
  AND REGEXP_CONTAINS(LOWER(email_log_info.message_info.subject), r'urgent|wire|transfer|payment|banking|invoice|confidential')
  AND email_log_info.disposition != 'SPAM'
ORDER BY
  event_info.timestamp DESC
LIMIT 100;
